serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (one plate), which is a typical restaurant or hawker portion of Vietnamese fried egg rice.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Egg Rice (Cơm Chiên Trứng Ốp La) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Egg Rice (Cơm Chiên Trứng Ốp La), cr_id G00010566, with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 1.1 g/100g based on typical egg-and-oil fried rice composition per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Item 35146, Fried rice with egg). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB and set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice, fried (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese Fried Egg Rice (Cơm Chiên Trứng Ốp La). GI of 72 is estimated from the closest analogous food: white fried rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 72 × 75.57 g (25.19 g/100g × 300 g serving) / 100 = 54.4. The presence of egg, oil, and dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g) may modestly lower the effective GI in practice.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g; borderline intake with high potassium 327 mg/100g partially offsetting pressor effect
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced soy sauce to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal, while the high potassium content (327 mg/100g) provides a partial DASH-diet benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~72) white rice base with carbohydrate 25.19 g/100g; dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g partially moderates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 150 g or fewer, substitute brown rice where possible to lower the GI, avoid sweet sauces, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat 4.21 g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~1.1 g/100g; sodium 231 mg/100g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g by HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose smaller portions (150–200 g), request the dish be prepared with minimal oil and no added soy sauce, and note that the dietary fibre content (3.7 g/100g) supports LDL reduction when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g supports gut microbiome and immune modulation; low saturated fat; soy sauce adds sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume this dish in moderate portions; request reduced soy sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling and prefer anti-inflammatory oils such as olive oil for preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Egg Rice (Cơm Chiên Trứng Ốp La) is a popular Vietnamese rice dish consisting of day-old cooked white rice stir-fried in a wok or pan with garlic, spring onion, soy sauce, and cooking oil, topped with a sunny-side up fried egg (ốp la). The dish is a staple of Vietnamese home cooking and street-food culture, commonly served for lunch or dinner. The rice is lightly seasoned and has a slightly crispy texture on the exterior with a soft interior, while the egg adds richness and protein. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with a notable dietary fibre content of 3.7 g and sodium of 231 mg. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but requires sodium monitoring for those with hypertension and carbohydrate monitoring for those with Type 2 Diabetes. It is egg-containing and not suitable for vegans; it is generally considered halal. The dish is widely available in Vietnamese restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore and across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010566, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy closest analogue and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (white fried rice, GI 72) and documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.1 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy Item 35146 (Fried rice with egg) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry; this is documented in nutrient_source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Vietnamese fried egg rice preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by the HPB source (confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010566); this is notable given the egg content and may reflect the analytical method or preparation variant used by HPB — consumers should be aware that a fried egg typically contributes cholesterol and this value should be interpreted with caution. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (white fried rice); no direct GI measurement for Cơm Chiên Trứng Ốp La was found in approved sources. 5. Vietnamese dish — mandatory Vietnamese local name is present; Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are not applicable for this cuisine category.
